answer: The method uses a point-based event representation, treating every moment in the video as a potential event candidate.
A video is first represented as a sequence of feature vectors, where each vector is derived from a short clip using pre-trained backbones.
The model's regression head predicts the temporal boundaries of an event by calculating the distances from an identified foreground point to the event's start (onset) and end (offset).
quotes: "Our solution inherits the point-based event representation from our prior work on temporal action localization , and develops a Transformer-based model for video grounding." (Abstract)
"The resulting model considers every moment in the video as an event candidate, measures their relevance to the text query, and regresses the event boundaries from foreground moments." (p. 1)
"Our method represents a video as a sequence of feature vectors, where each vector is derived from a short clip using pre-trained video backbones." (Sec 2)
"...the regression head predicts the distances from a foreground point to the event's onset and offset." (Sec 2)

paper_file: Audio Does Matter Importance-Aware Multi-Granularity Fusion for Video Moment Retrieval.md

answer: The paper addresses the task of Video Moment Retrieval (VMR), which aims to locate a specific segment within an untrimmed video that corresponds to a natural language query.
The goal is to predict the start and end frames of the relevant moment.

The paper discusses two general approaches: proposal-based methods, which pre-segment candidate clips, and proposal-free methods, which directly process video features. The proposed IMG model is a proposal-free approach.
quotes: "Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) aims to retrieve a specific moment semantically related to the given query." (Abstract)

"Video moment retrieval aims to retrieve the start-end frame pair {fs, fe} of a specific segment that semantically match the textual query Q = {wi}N i=1 from the untrimmed video V = {ft}T t=1" (Sec 3.1)

"Current approaches fall into two categories: proposal-based and proposal-free. For proposal-based [34, 47, 49, 57, 58, 72, 73, 75], it is often necessary to pre-segment the candidate proposals... For proposal-free [12, 22, 62, 63, 68–70, 74], they eliminate the need for predefined proposals, processing raw visual and textual features directly through cross-modal matching." (Sec 2)

answer: The model processes a maximum of 128 frames per video. The paper does not specify the sampling strategy used to select these frames (e.g., uniform, dense).
quotes: "For all datasets, we set the initial learning rate to 0.0005, and the maximum number of frames to 128." (Sec D)

answer: Target video length(s): Not mentioned. Reason: The paper does not provide statistics on the duration of the source videos in the datasets used.
Per-video processed duration(s): The model processes a feature sequence corresponding to a maximum of 128 frames.
If stated, longest video ≥ 60 minutes: No. Reason: The paper does not provide statistics on video durations.
quotes: "For all datasets, we set ... the maximum number of frames to 128." (Sec D)

answer: Addresses motion/dynamics/frame interaction: Yes
Model(s):
The model uses pre-trained visual backbones designed for video understanding, such as I3D, C3D, SlowFast, and InternVideo2 (IV2), which capture spatiotemporal features.

The proposed Multi-Granularity Fusion (MGF) module processes temporal sequences of features using 1D convolutional networks for local-level fusion, a slot attention mechanism to aggregate clips into events for event-level fusion, and Bi-GRUs to model relationships between features of different granularities.
quotes: "For vision modality, we extract the original visual features V ∈ RT×dv by a pre-trained visual CNN [7, 52, 76]" (Sec 3.2)

"Local-Level Visual-Audio Fusion. As shown in Figure 3(a), to match the visual and audio context frame-to-clip for fine-level fusion, we construct symmetric multi-kernel 1D convolutional networks and to deeply perceive the local relationships between video frames and audio clips" (Sec 3.3.2)

"Event-Level Visual-Audio Fusion. As shown in Figure 3(b), to match the event-aware semantics between vision and audio for activity reasoning, our event-level fusion module first employs a group of slot attention mechanism [37] to aggregate similar visual/audio clips into multiple events" (Sec 3.3.2)
"We use I3D [7] as pretrained visual features on all datasets." (Sec D)

answer: Visual-only analysis: Yes

Model(s): The framework includes a dedicated visual branch that operates independently of the audio and fusion branches. This branch processes visual features (from backbones like I3D, CLIP+SlowFast, or InternVideo2) and text features through a visual encoder and a vision-text fusion module (context-query attention) to predict moment boundaries. This branch can be used for inference when audio is unavailable.
quotes: "we propose a novel Importance-aware Multi-Granularity fusion model (IMG) with three prediction branches: audio branch, visual branch, and audio-visual fusion branch." (Sec 3.1)
"the visual-only feature and audio-only feature are also fed into their respective unimodal predictors." (Sec 3.1)
"In real-world scenarios, the audio modality may sometimes be irrelevant or unavailable, such as in surveillance footage. In such cases, the visual branch of IMG can still be employed" (Sec 4.3)

answer: Speech audio used: No

ASR/LLM/audio model(s): Not mentioned. Reason: The paper focuses on non-speech audio events like "laughing" or "closing the door" and does not mention using Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) or analyzing transcribed speech.
quotes: None.

answer: Non-speech audio (music/effects/background): Yes
Audio feature/model(s):
Pre-trained audio-aware CNNs are used to extract initial audio features.
For Charades-STA and Charades-AudioMatter datasets, PANN (pretrained on AudioSet) is used.
For the ActivityNet Captions dataset, VGGish (pretrained on YouTube-100M) is used.
These features are then processed by an audio encoder composed of FFN, convolutional, and transformer layers.
quotes: "Not all audios are helpful for video moment retrieval, and the audio of some videos may be complete noise or background sound that is meaningless to the moment determination." (Abstract)
"For audio modality, we firstly use pre-trained audio-aware CNN [21, 27] to extract its original features A ∈ RT×da" (Sec 3.2)

"For the audio pretraining models, following previous work [8], we utilized PANN [27], pretrained on AudioSet [17] dataset, and VGGish [21], pretrained on YouTube-100M [21] dataset, for Charades-STA/Charades-AudioMatter and ActivityNet Caption, respectively." (Sec D)

answer: Interactive Q&A/chat: No

LLM(s): Not mentioned. Reason: The task is video moment retrieval from a single query, not a conversational or interactive Q&A session. While LLaMA is mentioned as a potential text backbone, it is not used in an interactive chat capacity.
quotes: "Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) aims to retrieve a specific video segment based on a natural language query." (Sec 2)

answer: Retrieval surfacing: clips | timestamps

Retrieval model/framework(s): The proposed model is the Importance-aware Multi-Granularity fusion model (IMG). It uses a moment predictor head to output start and end logits over the video's timeline, defining the retrieved clip's boundaries.
quotes: "Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) aims to retrieve a specific moment semantically related to the given query." (Abstract)
"Video moment retrieval aims to retrieve the start-end frame pair {fs, fe} of a specific segment" (Sec 3.1)
"Following previous works [69], we exploit the moment predictor as the retrieval heads to output the start logits and end logits of the moment, and get the final prediction Ps and Pe." (Sec 3.5)

answer: How roles/activities are inferred: Activities are inferred by learning a deep, cross-modal alignment between the text query and audio-visual content. The model uses context-query attention to create text-guided audio and visual features. An Audio Importance Predictor (AIP) then calculates a weight to determine the audio's contribution. Finally, a Multi-Granularity Fusion (MGF) module combines the audio and visual features at local, event, and global levels. This fused representation is fed to a predictor that outputs start and end probabilities for the activity's timespan. The inference is learned end-to-end rather than through explicit symbolic rules.
Model/framework(s): Importance-aware Multi-Granularity fusion model (IMG), which includes an Audio Importance Predictor (AIP) and a Multi-Granularity Fusion (MGF) module.
quotes: "we employ interactions between vision-text and audiotext pairs, fusing them to derive text-semantic-activated visual and audio features." (Sec 3.1)
"an audio importance predictor generates a sample-wise score, which serves as a crucial weight to determine the audio-to-vision complementarity coefficients for the given sample pair." (Sec 3.1)

"the visual and audio features will be fed into a multi-granularity fusion module to aggregate the target-moment-related information at local-, event-, and global-levels according to the previously obtained important weight." (Sec 3.1)

answer: Indexing/representation: embeddings

Model/framework(s): Videos and their corresponding audio are represented as sequences of dense feature vectors (embeddings). Initial visual features are extracted using models like I3D, SlowFast, or InternVideo2. Initial audio features are extracted using PANN or VGGish. These pre-extracted embeddings are then mapped into a common latent space and processed by the IMG model's encoders and fusion modules to generate task-specific representations for moment retrieval.
quotes: "Given the pre-extracted visual, audio, and text features by the corresponding encoder, our IMG model first employs feed-forward network (FFN) layers to map these features into a common latent space." (Sec 3.1)
"For audio modality, we firstly use pre-trained audio-aware CNN [21, 27] to extract its original features A ∈ RT×da" (Sec 3.2)
"For vision modality, we extract the original visual features V ∈ RT×dv by a pre-trained visual CNN [7, 52, 76]" (Sec 3.2)

answer: Pre-extracted visual features and text query embeddings → Visual Encoder and Text Encoder → Vision-Text Fusion (Context-Query Attention) → Visual-only Predictor → Visual-only start/end logits.
Pre-extracted audio features and text query embeddings → Audio Encoder and Text Encoder → Audio-Text Fusion (Context-Query Attention) → Audio-only Predictor → Audio-only start/end logits.
Text-guided visual and audio features → Audio Importance Predictor (AIP) → Audio importance score `p`.
Text-guided visual and audio features → Multi-Granularity Fusion (MGF) module (weighted by score `p`) → Fused audio-visual features → Fusion Predictor → Fused start/end logits.
quotes: "Given the pre-extracted visual, audio, and text features by the corresponding encoder, our IMG model first employs feed-forward network (FFN) layers to map these features into a common latent space. Then, we employ interactions between vision-text and audiotext pairs, fusing them to derive text-semantic-activated visual and audio features. These features are then passed into the visual-audio fusion branch... an audio importance predictor generates a sample-wise score... Then, the visual and audio features will be fed into a multi-granularity fusion module... Finally, the three-level features are concatenated and fed into the predictor to output predictions, while the visual-only feature and audio-only feature are also fed into their respective unimodal predictors." (Sec 3.1)

answer: Public datasets: Charades-STA, ActivityNet Captions.

Proprietary or newly created datasets: Charades-AudioMatter, a new test set created by the authors. It consists of 1,196 samples manually selected from the Charades-STA test set where audio cues are particularly important for retrieval.

1) video_segmentation
Answer –
- The paper addresses the task of Video Moment Retrieval (VMR), which aims to locate a specific segment within an untrimmed video that corresponds to a natural language query.
- The goal is to predict the start and end frames of the relevant moment.
- The paper discusses two general approaches: proposal-based methods, which pre-segment candidate clips, and proposal-free methods, which directly process video features. The proposed IMG model is a proposal-free approach.
Quotes –
- "Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) aims to retrieve a specific moment semantically related to the given query." (Abstract)
- "Video moment retrieval aims to retrieve the start-end frame pair {fs, fe} of a specific segment that semantically match the textual query Q = {wi}N i=1 from the untrimmed video V = {ft}T t=1" (Sec 3.1)
- "Current approaches fall into two categories: proposal-based and proposal-free. For proposal-based [34, 47, 49, 57, 58, 72, 73, 75], it is often necessary to pre-segment the candidate proposals... For proposal-free [12, 22, 62, 63, 68–70, 74], they eliminate the need for predefined proposals, processing raw visual and textual features directly through cross-modal matching." (Sec 2)

2) frame_sampling_method
Answer –
- The model processes a maximum of 128 frames per video. The paper does not specify the sampling strategy used to select these frames (e.g., uniform, dense).
Quotes –
- "For all datasets, we set the initial learning rate to 0.0005, and the maximum number of frames to 128." (Sec D)

3) input_video_length
Answer –
- Target video length(s): Not mentioned. Reason: The paper does not provide statistics on the duration of the source videos in the datasets used.
- Per-video processed duration(s): The model processes a feature sequence corresponding to a maximum of 128 frames.
- If stated, longest video ≥ 60 minutes: No. Reason: The paper does not provide statistics on video durations.
Quotes –
- "For all datasets, we set ... the maximum number of frames to 128." (Sec D)

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer –
- Addresses motion/dynamics/frame interaction: Yes
- Model(s):
  - The model uses pre-trained visual backbones designed for video understanding, such as I3D, C3D, SlowFast, and InternVideo2 (IV2), which capture spatiotemporal features.
  - The proposed Multi-Granularity Fusion (MGF) module processes temporal sequences of features using 1D convolutional networks for local-level fusion, a slot attention mechanism to aggregate clips into events for event-level fusion, and Bi-GRUs to model relationships between features of different granularities.
Quotes –
- "For vision modality, we extract the original visual features V ∈ RT×dv by a pre-trained visual CNN [7, 52, 76]" (Sec 3.2)
- "Local-Level Visual-Audio Fusion. As shown in Figure 3(a), to match the visual and audio context frame-to-clip for fine-level fusion, we construct symmetric multi-kernel 1D convolutional networks and to deeply perceive the local relationships between video frames and audio clips" (Sec 3.3.2)
- "Event-Level Visual-Audio Fusion. As shown in Figure 3(b), to match the event-aware semantics between vision and audio for activity reasoning, our event-level fusion module first employs a group of slot attention mechanism [37] to aggregate similar visual/audio clips into multiple events" (Sec 3.3.2)
- "We use I3D [7] as pretrained visual features on all datasets." (Sec D)

5) visual_analysis
Answer –
- Visual-only analysis: Yes
- Model(s): The framework includes a dedicated visual branch that operates independently of the audio and fusion branches. This branch processes visual features (from backbones like I3D, CLIP+SlowFast, or InternVideo2) and text features through a visual encoder and a vision-text fusion module (context-query attention) to predict moment boundaries. This branch can be used for inference when audio is unavailable.
Quotes –
- "we propose a novel Importance-aware Multi-Granularity fusion model (IMG) with three prediction branches: audio branch, visual branch, and audio-visual fusion branch." (Sec 3.1)
- "the visual-only feature and audio-only feature are also fed into their respective unimodal predictors." (Sec 3.1)
- "In real-world scenarios, the audio modality may sometimes be irrelevant or unavailable, such as in surveillance footage. In such cases, the visual branch of IMG can still be employed" (Sec 4.3)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer –
- Speech audio used: No
- ASR/LLM/audio model(s): Not mentioned. Reason: The paper focuses on non-speech audio events like "laughing" or "closing the door" and does not mention using Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) or analyzing transcribed speech.
Quotes –
- None.

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer –
- Non-speech audio (music/effects/background): Yes
- Audio feature/model(s):
  - Pre-trained audio-aware CNNs are used to extract initial audio features.
  - For Charades-STA and Charades-AudioMatter datasets, PANN (pretrained on AudioSet) is used.
  - For the ActivityNet Captions dataset, VGGish (pretrained on YouTube-100M) is used.
  - These features are then processed by an audio encoder composed of FFN, convolutional, and transformer layers.
Quotes –
- "Not all audios are helpful for video moment retrieval, and the audio of some videos may be complete noise or background sound that is meaningless to the moment determination." (Abstract)
- "For audio modality, we firstly use pre-trained audio-aware CNN [21, 27] to extract its original features A ∈ RT×da" (Sec 3.2)
- "For the audio pretraining models, following previous work [8], we utilized PANN [27], pretrained on AudioSet [17] dataset, and VGGish [21], pretrained on YouTube-100M [21] dataset, for Charades-STA/Charades-AudioMatter and ActivityNet Caption, respectively." (Sec D)

8) qa_interaction
Answer –
- Interactive Q&A/chat: No

9) retrieval_level
Answer –
- Retrieval surfacing: clips | timestamps
- Retrieval model/framework(s): The proposed model is the Importance-aware Multi-Granularity fusion model (IMG). It uses a moment predictor head to output start and end logits over the video's timeline, defining the retrieved clip's boundaries.
Quotes –
- "Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) aims to retrieve a specific moment semantically related to the given query." (Abstract)
- "Video moment retrieval aims to retrieve the start-end frame pair {fs, fe} of a specific segment" (Sec 3.1)
- "Following previous works [69], we exploit the moment predictor as the retrieval heads to output the start logits and end logits of the moment, and get the final prediction Ps and Pe." (Sec 3.5)

10) inferring_method
Answer –
- How roles/activities are inferred: Activities are inferred by learning a deep, cross-modal alignment between the text query and audio-visual content. The model uses context-query attention to create text-guided audio and visual features. An Audio Importance Predictor (AIP) then calculates a weight to determine the audio's contribution. Finally, a Multi-Granularity Fusion (MGF) module combines the audio and visual features at local, event, and global levels. This fused representation is fed to a predictor that outputs start and end probabilities for the activity's timespan. The inference is learned end-to-end rather than through explicit symbolic rules.
- Model/framework(s): Importance-aware Multi-Granularity fusion model (IMG), which includes an Audio Importance Predictor (AIP) and a Multi-Granularity Fusion (MGF) module.
Quotes –
- "we employ interactions between vision-text and audiotext pairs, fusing them to derive text-semantic-activated visual and audio features." (Sec 3.1)
- "an audio importance predictor generates a sample-wise score, which serves as a crucial weight to determine the audio-to-vision complementarity coefficients for the given sample pair." (Sec 3.1)
- "the visual and audio features will be fed into a multi-granularity fusion module to aggregate the target-moment-related information at local-, event-, and global-levels according to the previously obtained important weight." (Sec 3.1)

11) video_representation
Answer –
- Indexing/representation: embeddings
- Model/framework(s): Videos and their corresponding audio are represented as sequences of dense feature vectors (embeddings). Initial visual features are extracted using models like I3D, SlowFast, or InternVideo2. Initial audio features are extracted using PANN or VGGish. These pre-extracted embeddings are then mapped into a common latent space and processed by the IMG model's encoders and fusion modules to generate task-specific representations for moment retrieval.
Quotes –
- "Given the pre-extracted visual, audio, and text features by the corresponding encoder, our IMG model first employs feed-forward network (FFN) layers to map these features into a common latent space." (Sec 3.1)
- "For audio modality, we firstly use pre-trained audio-aware CNN [21, 27] to extract its original features A ∈ RT×da" (Sec 3.2)
- "For vision modality, we extract the original visual features V ∈ RT×dv by a pre-trained visual CNN [7, 52, 76]" (Sec 3.2)

12) model_pipeline
Answer –
- Pre-extracted visual features and text query embeddings → Visual Encoder and Text Encoder → Vision-Text Fusion (Context-Query Attention) → Visual-only Predictor → Visual-only start/end logits.
- Pre-extracted audio features and text query embeddings → Audio Encoder and Text Encoder → Audio-Text Fusion (Context-Query Attention) → Audio-only Predictor → Audio-only start/end logits.
- Text-guided visual and audio features → Audio Importance Predictor (AIP) → Audio importance score `p`.
- Text-guided visual and audio features → Multi-Granularity Fusion (MGF) module (weighted by score `p`) → Fused audio-visual features → Fusion Predictor → Fused start/end logits.
Quotes –
- "Given the pre-extracted visual, audio, and text features by the corresponding encoder, our IMG model first employs feed-forward network (FFN) layers to map these features into a common latent space. Then, we employ interactions between vision-text and audiotext pairs, fusing them to derive text-semantic-activated visual and audio features. These features are then passed into the visual-audio fusion branch... an audio importance predictor generates a sample-wise score... Then, the visual and audio features will be fed into a multi-granularity fusion module... Finally, the three-level features are concatenated and fed into the predictor to output predictions, while the visual-only feature and audio-only feature are also fed into their respective unimodal predictors." (Sec 3.1)

answer: The primary method samples videos at 1 frame per second (FPS) and extracts up to 10 random frames from each video for the data curation pipeline.

The AudioCLIP baseline was trained on single random frames from videos.

answer: Retrieval surfacing: clips

Retrieval model/framework(s): A contrastive learning-based model is used for retrieval. It computes the cosine similarity between the embedding of a query video frame (from a frozen CLIP image encoder) and the embeddings of a database of SFX audio files (from a trained audio encoder like PANNS or Wav2CLIP).
quotes: "Once trained, the model can be used for retrieval by computing the embedding distance between a query frame and a dataset of SFX to rank the SFX by their similarity to the visual query." (p. 1, Section 2.2)

answer: How roles/activities are inferred (e.g., “person in car → driving”): The system does not infer high-level activities. It learns a direct, shared embedding space for visual content and sound effects through contrastive learning. Retrieval is based on the learned correspondence and proximity (cosine similarity) within this space, rather than explicit logical inference.
Model/framework(s): Contrastive learning framework with a CLIP image encoder and a trainable audio encoder (PANNS or Wav2CLIP).

answer: Indexing/representation: embeddings

quotes: "...we use contrastive learning with a standard contrastive loss to train an audio encoder to project an audio signal into the CLIP image embedding space." (p. 1, Section 2.2)
"We do so by keeping the original CLIP image encoder frozen during training, forcing the audio encoder to project audio into this pre-trained space." (p. 1, Section 2.2)

answer: Data Curation: SFX text tags → Bloom LLM → Natural language sentences → CLIP text encoder → Text embeddings. Video frames → CLIP image encoder → Image embeddings. Text and image embeddings are matched via cosine similarity to create audio-visual training pairs.

Retrieval: A query video frame → Frozen CLIP image encoder → Visual embedding. A database of SFX audio files → Trained audio encoder (PANNS or Wav2CLIP) → Audio embeddings. The visual embedding is compared against all audio embeddings using cosine similarity to produce a ranked list of relevant SFX.
quotes: "First, we use text metadata associated with each SFX to generate text embeddings via CLIP [25], and in parallel generate CLIP image embeddings of video frames. Next, we retrieve the most similar visual frames to each SFX-based text query via cosine similarity." (p. 1, Section 2.1)

This curated data is split into training, validation, and test sets (e.g., PSE-Test). A separate test set, ASFX-Test, was created using the public Adobe Audition SFX library and the same proprietary stock videos.

Answer –
- The primary method samples videos at 1 frame per second (FPS) and extracts up to 10 random frames from each video for the data curation pipeline.
- The Wav2CLIP baseline was trained on 5-second video chunks sampled at a high frame rate, with frames being mean-pooled to get segment-level embeddings.
- The AudioCLIP baseline was trained on single random frames from videos.
Quotes –
- "We sample the videos at 1FPS, and extract up to 10 random frames per video, yielding approximately 1.1 million video frames to use in our automatic data curation pipeline." (p. 1, Section 2.1)
- "Wav2CLIP uses a ResNet18 architecture for the audio encoder and was trained on 5-second chunks of video frames from VGGSound [11] sampled at a high frame rate and mean-pooled for segment-level CLIP embeddings." (p. 2, Section 3.3)
- "AudioCLIP... was trained on single random frames from AudioSet [18] videos." (p. 2, Section 3.3)

3) input_video_length
Answer –

- Per-video processed duration(s): The source videos used for creating the training data are "mostly 10 seconds or less in duration".
- If stated, longest video ≥ 60 minutes: No
Quotes –

4) spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer –

- Model(s): The system uses a static frame as a query and does not analyze motion. The authors state that extending the system to use video clips and leverage motion cues is planned for future work.
Quotes –
- "For future work we plan to extend our system to utilize video clips as queries instead of single video frames, which will enable us to leverage the motion cues in the video in addition to the semantic cues contained in a static frame for SFX retrieval." (p. 3, Section 5)

5) visual_analysis
Answer –
- Visual-only analysis: Yes
- Model(s): The system uses a frozen CLIP image encoder to generate embeddings from a single video frame, which serves as the query for retrieving sound effects.
Quotes –
- "...we use a given video frame as query to rank the audio from all test pairs based on their cosine similarity in the multimodal embedding space." (p. 2, Section 3.2)
- "We do so by keeping the original CLIP image encoder frozen during training, forcing the audio encoder to project audio into this pre-trained space." (p. 1, Section 2.2)

6) speech_audio_analysis
Answer –
- Speech audio used: No
- ASR/LLM/audio model(s): The datasets were explicitly filtered to exclude human sounds like speech and crying.
Quotes –
- "For audio, we removed human sounds (e.g., speech, crying) and music (e.g., saxophone, orchestra)." (p. 1, Section 2.1)
- "We filter AVE to exclude videos containing human sounds (notably speech) or music, which as noted earlier are outside the scope of this work, leaving 2.4k videos across 17 categories." (p. 2, Section 3.1)

7) sound_audio_analysis
Answer –
- Non-speech audio (music/effects/background): Yes
- Audio feature/model(s): The system is designed to retrieve sound effects (SFX). It uses a trainable audio encoder to map audio into a shared embedding space. The architectures tested for the audio encoder are PANNS and the ResNet18 from Wav2CLIP.
Quotes –
- "Finding the right sound effects (SFX) to match moments in a video is a difficult and time-consuming task..." (Abstract)
- "For our system, we experiment with two model architectures for the audio encoder: PANNS [31] and Wav2CLIP [13]." (p. 2, Section 3.4)

8) qa_interaction
Answer –
- Interactive Q&A/chat: No
- LLM(s): The system is a cross-modal retrieval framework that takes a video frame as input and outputs a ranked list of sound effects. It is not an interactive chat or Q&A system. An LLM (Bloom) is used in the offline data curation pipeline, but not for user interaction.
Quotes –
- "Retrieving high-quality (HQ) SFX using a video frame directly as the query is an attractive alternative, removing the reliance on text metadata..." (Abstract)

9) retrieval_level
Answer –
- Retrieval surfacing: clips
- Retrieval model/framework(s): A contrastive learning-based model is used for retrieval. It computes the cosine similarity between the embedding of a query video frame (from a frozen CLIP image encoder) and the embeddings of a database of SFX audio files (from a trained audio encoder like PANNS or Wav2CLIP).
Quotes –
- "Once trained, the model can be used for retrieval by computing the embedding distance between a query frame and a dataset of SFX to rank the SFX by their similarity to the visual query." (p. 1, Section 2.2)

10) inferring_method
Answer –
- How roles/activities are inferred (e.g., “person in car → driving”): The system does not infer high-level activities. It learns a direct, shared embedding space for visual content and sound effects through contrastive learning. Retrieval is based on the learned correspondence and proximity (cosine similarity) within this space, rather than explicit logical inference.
- Model/framework(s): Contrastive learning framework with a CLIP image encoder and a trainable audio encoder (PANNS or Wav2CLIP).
Quotes –
- "We use our HQ audio-visual training pairs to train a multimodal model that maps audio and video frames into a shared embedding space (Figure 1, right)." (p. 1, Section 2.2)

11) video_representation
Answer –
- Indexing/representation: embeddings
- Model/framework(s): Video frames are represented as embeddings generated by a frozen CLIP image encoder. The system's goal is to train an audio encoder (e.g., PANNS) to project audio signals into this same pre-trained CLIP image embedding space.
Quotes –
- "...we use contrastive learning with a standard contrastive loss to train an audio encoder to project an audio signal into the CLIP image embedding space." (p. 1, Section 2.2)
- "We do so by keeping the original CLIP image encoder frozen during training, forcing the audio encoder to project audio into this pre-trained space." (p. 1, Section 2.2)

12) model_pipeline
Answer –
- Data Curation: SFX text tags → Bloom LLM → Natural language sentences → CLIP text encoder → Text embeddings. Video frames → CLIP image encoder → Image embeddings. Text and image embeddings are matched via cosine similarity to create audio-visual training pairs.
- Retrieval: A query video frame → Frozen CLIP image encoder → Visual embedding. A database of SFX audio files → Trained audio encoder (PANNS or Wav2CLIP) → Audio embeddings. The visual embedding is compared against all audio embeddings using cosine similarity to produce a ranked list of relevant SFX.
Quotes –
- "First, we use text metadata associated with each SFX to generate text embeddings via CLIP [25], and in parallel generate CLIP image embeddings of video frames. Next, we retrieve the most similar visual frames to each SFX-based text query via cosine similarity." (p. 1, Section 2.1)
- "Once trained, the model can be used for retrieval by computing the embedding distance between a query frame and a dataset of SFX to rank the SFX by their similarity to the visual query." (p. 1, Section 2.2)

answer: Retrieval surfacing: videos | clips

Retrieval model/framework(s): The primary task is retrieving relevant documents (videos). For the downstream long-video QA task, the system retrieves relevant segments or key frames (clips) from within a video. The retrieval model is CLAMR, which is based on a contextualized late-interaction framework.
quotes: "Given a query q, the retriever must identify the most relevant document d." (p. 3, Sec. 3.1)
"...given a query about a long (up to ∼ 60 minute) video, we use CLAMR to retrieve relevant segments." (p. 2, Sec. 1)
"We propose CLAMR (Contextualized Late-interaction for Multimodal content Retrieval), a novel contextualized late-interaction multimodal retrieval framework..." (p. 2, Sec. 3)

answer: How roles/activities are inferred (e.g., “person in car → driving”): The model does not use an explicit reasoning module for inferring high-level activities. Instead, relevance is determined by matching query token embeddings to document token/patch embeddings from all modalities using a late-interaction mechanism. This fine-grained similarity matching, learned via a contrastive objective, implicitly captures the relationship between the query and the multimodal content.
Model/framework(s): CLAMR, using a modality-wise late interaction similarity score and a multimodal contrastive learning objective.
quotes: "Late interaction (LI) [19, 31, 12] compares token-level embeddings instead of pooled embeddings: for each query token, its maximum cosine similarity to any document token is computed, and these maximum similarities are then summed over all query tokens." (p. 3, Sec. 3.3)

"Our goal is to train the model to not only retrieve the correct document but also dynamically select the optimal modalities. ... We use the standard InfoNCE loss [36] to train the model to retrieve the correct document from a batch that includes other negative documents." (p. 4, Sec. 3.4)

answer: Indexing/representation: embeddings

Model/framework(s): The system uses a late-interaction approach (inspired by ColBERT) where each document is represented by multiple token- and patch-level embeddings rather than a single pooled vector. Each modality within a document is represented by its own matrix of embeddings, allowing for fine-grained, token-level similarity computations. The model that generates these representations is CLAMR.
quotes: "Each document provides one embedding matrix per modality $\mathbf{E}_{d,m} \in \mathbb{R}^{N_{d,m} \times D}$ for $m \in \mathcal{M}$." (p. 3, Sec. 3.3)
"...late-interaction models preserve multiple embeddings per item for detailed matching." (p. 2, Sec. 2)

"Unlike standard dual encoder retrievers that match queries and documents via fast but coarse-grained similarity in a shared embedding space... late-interaction methods offer a middle-ground by enabling fine-grained token-level matching..." (p. 2, Sec. 2)

answer: Training: A text query and a multimodal document (video frames, ASR, OCR, metadata) are concatenated and fed into a VLM backbone (e.g., Qwen-VL-2.5-3B) to produce contextualized token/patch embeddings. A modality-wise late interaction score is computed, and the model is trained with an InfoNCE contrastive loss to distinguish positive from negative documents.

Retrieval Inference: A text query and a set of candidate documents are encoded by the trained CLAMR model. A late-interaction similarity score is computed for each query-document pair, and the documents are ranked based on these scores to produce the final retrieval list.

Long-Video QA RAG: A long video and a text question are provided. CLAMR retrieves the most relevant key frames from the video. These frames, along with the original question, are then passed to a VLM answerer (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Inst) which generates the final answer.
quotes: "A text query and multimodal video document consisting of video's visual, audio, OCR, and metadata signals are encoded by the model. Then, token-level late interaction yields a similarity score for each modality; the highest of these scores becomes the query-document similarity. Similarities for the positive pair and in-batch negatives are fed to a contrastive loss." (p. 3, Figure 2 caption)

"...we set up a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline: given a long video, the retriever first selects key frames relevant to the question, which are subsequently provided as input to a VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Inst) answerer." (p. 9, Sec. 6.4)

answer: Baselines/competitors: Single-modality baselines include mCLIP, ImageBind, and LanguageBind on individual modalities (vision, audio, OCR, metadata). Multi-modality baselines include mCLIP, ImageBind, and LanguageBind using score averaging or a router, as well as a fine-tuned Qwen-VL 2.5 with pooled representations.
Datasets used for comparison: MULTIVENT 2.0++ and MSR-VTT for retrieval tasks. Video-MME and LongVideoBench for long-video QA.

Reported gains (metrics & deltas): On MULTIVENT 2.0++, CLAMR improves nDCG@10 by 25.6 points over the best single-modality retriever and 35.4 points over the best multi-modality retriever. On MSR-VTT, CLAMR (VLM) achieves 46.1% R@1, a 5.9% improvement over the strongest single-modality baseline (LanguageBind Vision). In long-video QA, CLAMR outperforms LanguageBind by 3.50% on Video-MME (with subtitles) and uniform sampling by 4.79% on LongVideoBench.
quotes: "on MULTIVENT 2.0++, CLAMR improves nDCG@10 by 25.6 points over the bestperforming single-modality retriever and by 35.4 points over the best-performing multi-modality retriever." (p. 1, Abstract)
"On MSR-VTT, CLAMR achieves an R@1 of 46.1%, surpassing the strongest single-modality baseline (LanguageBind Vision) by 5.9%." (p. 7, Sec. 6.1)

"On LongVideoBench, CLAMR achieves 57.09% accuracy, surpassing uniform sampling by 4.79%. On Video-MME, CLAMR outperforms LanguageBind by 1.50% without subtitles and 3.50% with subtitles." (p. 9, Sec. 6.4)

answer: Key hyperparameters (batch size, LR, epochs, frames, crop size, etc.): The model uses a batch size of 16, a learning rate of 1 × 10<sup>−5</sup>, and is trained for 1 epoch on MULTIVENT 2.0++ and 5 epochs on MSRVTT. It uses an 8-bit Adam optimizer. For video, it processes up to 10 key frames resized to 224 × 224 pixels. The model is quantized to 4-bits using QLoRA with a rank r=128 and α=128. The projection layer dimension is 128. Maximum query length is 64 tokens and maximum text modality length is 256 tokens.
quotes: "We train separate versions of CLAMR on MULTIVENT 2.0++ and MSRVTT for 1 and 5 epochs, respectively. Training is performed using a batch size of 16, distributed across 8 A100 80GB GPUs." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
"To reduce memory usage, we employ 4-bit quantization with QLoRA [9], setting the LoRA rank r = 128 and α = 128." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
"...train with the 8-bit Adam optimizer, and set the learning rate to 1 × 10<sup>−5</sup> for all experiments." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
"We append a 128-dimensional linear projection layer, following ColPali [12]." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
"For video input, we resize frames to 224 × 224 pixels..." (p. 15, Sec. B.1)

answer: GPUs/TPUs: Training was performed on 8 A100 80GB GPUs.
Training time: Approximately 10 hours on MULTIVENT 2.0++ and about 4 hours on MSRVTT.

- "We propose CLAMR (Contextualized Late-interaction for Multimodal content Retrieval), a novel contextualized late-interaction multimodal retrieval framework..." (p. 2, Sec. 3)

10) inferring_method
Answer –
- How roles/activities are inferred (e.g., “person in car → driving”): The model does not use an explicit reasoning module for inferring high-level activities. Instead, relevance is determined by matching query token embeddings to document token/patch embeddings from all modalities using a late-interaction mechanism. This fine-grained similarity matching, learned via a contrastive objective, implicitly captures the relationship between the query and the multimodal content.
- Model/framework(s): CLAMR, using a modality-wise late interaction similarity score and a multimodal contrastive learning objective.
Quotes –
- "Late interaction (LI) [19, 31, 12] compares token-level embeddings instead of pooled embeddings: for each query token, its maximum cosine similarity to any document token is computed, and these maximum similarities are then summed over all query tokens." (p. 3, Sec. 3.3)
- "Our goal is to train the model to not only retrieve the correct document but also dynamically select the optimal modalities. ... We use the standard InfoNCE loss [36] to train the model to retrieve the correct document from a batch that includes other negative documents." (p. 4, Sec. 3.4)

11) video_representation
Answer –
- Indexing/representation: embeddings
- Model/framework(s): The system uses a late-interaction approach (inspired by ColBERT) where each document is represented by multiple token- and patch-level embeddings rather than a single pooled vector. Each modality within a document is represented by its own matrix of embeddings, allowing for fine-grained, token-level similarity computations. The model that generates these representations is CLAMR.
Quotes –
- "Each document provides one embedding matrix per modality $\mathbf{E}_{d,m} \in \mathbb{R}^{N_{d,m} \times D}$ for $m \in \mathcal{M}$." (p. 3, Sec. 3.3)
- "...late-interaction models preserve multiple embeddings per item for detailed matching." (p. 2, Sec. 2)
- "Unlike standard dual encoder retrievers that match queries and documents via fast but coarse-grained similarity in a shared embedding space... late-interaction methods offer a middle-ground by enabling fine-grained token-level matching..." (p. 2, Sec. 2)

12) model_pipeline
Answer –
- Training: A text query and a multimodal document (video frames, ASR, OCR, metadata) are concatenated and fed into a VLM backbone (e.g., Qwen-VL-2.5-3B) to produce contextualized token/patch embeddings. A modality-wise late interaction score is computed, and the model is trained with an InfoNCE contrastive loss to distinguish positive from negative documents.
- Retrieval Inference: A text query and a set of candidate documents are encoded by the trained CLAMR model. A late-interaction similarity score is computed for each query-document pair, and the documents are ranked based on these scores to produce the final retrieval list.
- Long-Video QA RAG: A long video and a text question are provided. CLAMR retrieves the most relevant key frames from the video. These frames, along with the original question, are then passed to a VLM answerer (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Inst) which generates the final answer.
Quotes –
- "A text query and multimodal video document consisting of video's visual, audio, OCR, and metadata signals are encoded by the model. Then, token-level late interaction yields a similarity score for each modality; the highest of these scores becomes the query-document similarity. Similarities for the positive pair and in-batch negatives are fed to a contrastive loss." (p. 3, Figure 2 caption)
- "...we set up a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline: given a long video, the retriever first selects key frames relevant to the question, which are subsequently provided as input to a VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Inst) answerer." (p. 9, Sec. 6.4)

13) dataset
Answer –
- Public datasets: MULTIVENT 2.0, MSR-VTT, Video-MME, LongVideoBench.
- Proprietary or newly created datasets: MULTIVENT 2.0++, a new dataset created by synthetically generating 371k modality-specific queries for the unannotated videos in MULTIVENT 2.0.
- Links (if present in text): https://github.com/meetdavidwan/clamr
Quotes –
- "On the test sets of MULTIVENT 2.0++ and MSRVTT, we observe that conventional aggregation strategies... degrade performance..." (p. 1, Abstract)
- "We demonstrate the downstream benefits of CLAMR's improved retrieval ability on long-video question answering (QA), where... CLAMR provides improvements over LanguageBind on both VideoMME [13] and LongVideoBench [40]..." (p. 2, Sec. 1)
- "To address this limitation, we introduce MULTIVENT 2.0++ augmenting training queries for MULTIVENT 2.0 on the unannotated videos." (p. 5, Sec. 4)
- "Code and data are available in https://github.com/meetdavidwan/clamr." (p. 1, Footnote 1)

14) comparisons
Answer –
- Baselines/competitors: Single-modality baselines include mCLIP, ImageBind, and LanguageBind on individual modalities (vision, audio, OCR, metadata). Multi-modality baselines include mCLIP, ImageBind, and LanguageBind using score averaging or a router, as well as a fine-tuned Qwen-VL 2.5 with pooled representations.
- Datasets used for comparison: MULTIVENT 2.0++ and MSR-VTT for retrieval tasks. Video-MME and LongVideoBench for long-video QA.
- Reported gains (metrics & deltas): On MULTIVENT 2.0++, CLAMR improves nDCG@10 by 25.6 points over the best single-modality retriever and 35.4 points over the best multi-modality retriever. On MSR-VTT, CLAMR (VLM) achieves 46.1% R@1, a 5.9% improvement over the strongest single-modality baseline (LanguageBind Vision). In long-video QA, CLAMR outperforms LanguageBind by 3.50% on Video-MME (with subtitles) and uniform sampling by 4.79% on LongVideoBench.
Quotes –
- "on MULTIVENT 2.0++, CLAMR improves nDCG@10 by 25.6 points over the bestperforming single-modality retriever and by 35.4 points over the best-performing multi-modality retriever." (p. 1, Abstract)
- "On MSR-VTT, CLAMR achieves an R@1 of 46.1%, surpassing the strongest single-modality baseline (LanguageBind Vision) by 5.9%." (p. 7, Sec. 6.1)
- "On LongVideoBench, CLAMR achieves 57.09% accuracy, surpassing uniform sampling by 4.79%. On Video-MME, CLAMR outperforms LanguageBind by 1.50% without subtitles and 3.50% with subtitles." (p. 9, Sec. 6.4)

15) hyperparameters
Answer –
- Key hyperparameters (batch size, LR, epochs, frames, crop size, etc.): The model uses a batch size of 16, a learning rate of 1 × 10<sup>−5</sup>, and is trained for 1 epoch on MULTIVENT 2.0++ and 5 epochs on MSRVTT. It uses an 8-bit Adam optimizer. For video, it processes up to 10 key frames resized to 224 × 224 pixels. The model is quantized to 4-bits using QLoRA with a rank r=128 and α=128. The projection layer dimension is 128. Maximum query length is 64 tokens and maximum text modality length is 256 tokens.
Quotes –
- "We train separate versions of CLAMR on MULTIVENT 2.0++ and MSRVTT for 1 and 5 epochs, respectively. Training is performed using a batch size of 16, distributed across 8 A100 80GB GPUs." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
- "To reduce memory usage, we employ 4-bit quantization with QLoRA [9], setting the LoRA rank r = 128 and α = 128." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
- "...train with the 8-bit Adam optimizer, and set the learning rate to 1 × 10<sup>−5</sup> for all experiments." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
- "We append a 128-dimensional linear projection layer, following ColPali [12]." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
- "For video input, we resize frames to 224 × 224 pixels..." (p. 15, Sec. B.1)

16) environment
Answer –
- GPUs/TPUs: Training was performed on 8 A100 80GB GPUs.
- Training time: Approximately 10 hours on MULTIVENT 2.0++ and about 4 hours on MSRVTT.
- Other relevant compute details: The implementation is built on the *transformers* library.
Quotes –
- "Training is performed using a batch size of 16, distributed across 8 A100 80GB GPUs." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
- "Training on MULTIVENT 2.0++ required approximately 10 hours, while training on MSRVTT took about 4 hours." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)
- "Our implementation is built on the *transformers* library [39]." (p. 5, Sec. 5.1)

Answer –

video_segmentation
Answer –
- The paper uses pre-segmented "key scenes" or "clips" from movies, which are sourced from the MovieClips channel on YouTube.
- The dataset is described as providing a "condensed look at the full storyline" by focusing on these salient clips, which are ordered by their appearance time in the movie.
- The paper does not propose a new video segmentation method; it leverages the existing segmentation provided by the source channel.
Quotes –
- "Instead of considering the entire movie, we propose to learn from the 'key scenes' of the movie, providing a condensed look at the full storyline." (Abstract)
- "For each movie there is a set of ordered clips (typically 10 or so) covering the salient parts of the film..." (p. 4)
- "Raw videos are downloaded from YouTube. Each video is accompanied by an outro at the end of the clip which contains some advertising... This is automatically removed..." (p. 5)

frame_sampling_method
Answer –
- RGB object features: Frames are extracted at 25 fps.
- Scene features: Frames are extracted at 1 fps, using center crops.
- Face features: A face detector is run on each frame.
- Motion features: The paper follows the procedure described by the I3D model authors.
Quotes –
- "RGB object frame-level embeddings of the visual data are generated with an SENet-154 model... Frames are extracted at 25 fps, where each frame is resized to 224×224 pixels." (Sec. 5.3)
- "Scene features of 2208 dimensions are encoded using a DenseNet161 model... applied to 224×224 pixel centre crops of frames extracted at 1fps." (Sec. 5.3)
- "Face embeddings for each face track are extracted in three stages: (1) Each frame is passed through a dual shot face detector..." (Sec. 5.3)
- "Motion embeddings are generated using the I3D inception model... following the procedure described by [53]." (Sec. 5.3)

input_video_length
Answer –
- Target video length(s): The source videos are full-length movies, typically around 2 hours.
- Per-video processed duration(s): The processed units are "key scenes" or "clips" which have a median length of 132 seconds. Each movie is condensed to a total of roughly 20 minutes of key scenes.
- If stated, longest video ≥ 60 minutes: Yes. The source materials are movies, which are longer than 60 minutes, but the processed clips are much shorter.
Quotes –
- "...movies are very long (roughly 2 hours) and video architectures struggle to learn over such large timescales;" (Sec. 1)
- "Each around two minutes in length, the clips contain the same rich and complex story as full-length films but an order of magnitude shorter." (p. 4)
- "Median clip len. (secs): 132" (Table 2, p. 5)
- "...with just the key scenes, each movie has been condensed into roughly 20 minutes each." (p. 4)

spatiotemporal_analysis
Answer –

- Model(s): I3D inception model (pretrained on Kinetics) is used to generate motion embeddings.

visual_analysis
Answer –
- Visual-only analysis: Yes
- Model(s): The system uses a mixture of "expert" models for visual analysis:
- Objects: SENet-154 (pretrained on ImageNet) for RGB object embeddings.
- Faces: A dual shot face detector (DSFD) followed by an SENet50 (trained on VGGFace2) for face embeddings.
- Scenes: DenseNet161 (pretrained on Places365) for scene features.

retrieval_level
Answer –
- Retrieval surfacing: clips. The system retrieves "key scenes" or "video clips" from movies.
- Retrieval model/framework(s): The core frameworks are Mixture of Embedding Experts (MoEE) and Collaborative Experts (CE). The authors propose a Contextual Boost Module (CBM) to enhance these models by incorporating information from past and future clips.
Quotes –
- "The goal here is to retrieve the correct 'key scene' over all movies in the dataset, given just the high level description." (Sec. 4)
- "Henceforth, we use the term 'video clip' to refer to one key scene, and 'description' to refer to the high level semantic text accompanying each video clip." (Sec. 4)
- "Inspired by [13], we base our network architecture on a mixture of 'expert' embeddings model..." (Sec. 4)
- "Hence we introduce a Contextual Boost module (CBM), which allows the model to learn to increase the relative weight of a past video feature as well." (Sec. 4)

inferring_method
Answer –
- How roles/activities are inferred (e.g., “person in car → driving”): The system learns to associate high-level semantic text descriptions with multi-modal video embeddings. Inference is not based on explicit rules but is learned implicitly by the joint embedding model (MoEE or CE), which maps complex concepts in the text (like intent, emotion, relationships) to patterns in the combined visual, speech, and character features.
Quotes –
- "In order to achieve this task, we learn a common embedding space for each video and the description accompanying it." (Sec. 4)
- "Each descriptor is a semantic representation of the video learnt by individual experts (that encode concepts such as scenes, faces, actions, objects and the content of conversational speech from subtitles)." (Sec. 4)
- "This allows the model to learn to increase the relative weight of motion descriptors for input captions concerning human actions, or increase the relative weight of face descriptors for input captions that require detailed face understanding." (Sec. 4)

video_representation
Answer –
- Indexing/representation: embeddings. Each video clip is represented as a combination of embeddings from multiple "expert" streams, creating a holistic clip representation.
- Model/framework(s): The final video representation is a weighted combination of expert embeddings (face, subtitles, objects, motion, scene) produced by a Mixture of Embedding Experts (MoEE) or Collaborative Experts (CE) model. The weights are determined by the text query.
Quotes –
- "...we encode each video as a combination of different streams of descriptors. Each descriptor is a semantic representation of the video learnt by individual experts (that encode concepts such as scenes, faces, actions, objects and the content of conversational speech from subtitles)." (Sec. 4)
- "The final similarity score s is obtained by a weighted combination of the similarity scores... between the embeddings... of the query sentence T and the expert embeddings... (obtained from the input video descriptors...)." (Sec. 4.1)

model_pipeline
Answer –
- Input Video Clips → Pre-trained expert models (I3D, SENet-154, SENet50, DenseNet161) extract frame-level features for motion, objects, faces, and scenes, which are then aggregated. Subtitles are encoded with BERT. → These expert embeddings, along with embeddings from contextual (past/future) clips, are fed into a Mixture of Embedding Experts (MoEE) or Collaborative Experts (CE) model with a Contextual Boost Module (CBM).
- Input Text Query → The text is encoded using BERT and a NetVLAD layer. → The text embedding is used to predict weights for the different video expert embeddings (and contextual embeddings) and is also projected into the common embedding space. → The model computes a weighted similarity score between the text and video embeddings to retrieve the best matching video clip.
Quotes –
- "Model architecture: An overview of text-to-video retrieval with our Contextual Boost module (CBM) that computes a similarity score between a query sentence T and a target video. CBM receives contextual video features (which are previous clips from the same movie) to improve the multimodal encoding of the target video clip. The expert features are extracted using pre-trained models for speech, motion, faces, scenes and objects." (Fig. 4, p. 7)

dataset
Answer –
- Public datasets: Pre-training of expert models used ImageNet, Kinetics, VGGFace2, Wider Face, and Places365. The paper also compares its new dataset to many others, including LSMDC, MovieQA, MovieGraphs, AVA, and MovieNet.
- Proprietary or newly created datasets: The paper introduces the Condensed Movies Dataset (CMD), consisting of over 33,000 key scenes from 3,605 movies, sourced from the MovieClips YouTube channel. It includes video clips, semantic descriptions, character face-tracks, and movie metadata.
- Links (if present in text): https://www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~vgg/research/condensed-movies/
Quotes –

- "Data, code, models and features can be found at https://www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~vgg/research/condensed-movies/." (p. 2)

comparisons
Answer –
- Baselines/competitors: Random weights, E2EWS, Collaborative Experts (CE), and Mixture of Embedded Experts (MoEE). The proposed Contextual Boost Module (CBM) is added to CE and MoEE for comparison.
- Datasets used for comparison: All comparisons are performed on the newly created Condensed Movies Dataset (CMD).
- Reported gains (metrics & deltas): On cross-movie retrieval, MoEE + CBM improves over the MoEE baseline, increasing R@1 from 4.7 to 5.6, R@5 from 14.9 to 17.6, and reducing Median Rank from 65 to 50. On within-movie retrieval, adding a Character Module to MoEE increases m-R@1 from 38.9 to 47.2 (with track-freq norm).

- "Cross-movie text-video retrieval results on the CMD test set... MoEE [13] | 4.7 | 14.9 | 22.1 | 65 | 285.3... MoEE + CBM (ours) | 5.6 | 17.6 | 26.1 | 50 | 243.9" (Table 4, p. 11)
